# Solution for 121. Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock

**Generated by:** gpt-5
**Date:** 2026-01-23 13:31:09
**Source:** <https://leetcode.com/problems/best-time-to-buy-and-sell-stock/>

---

1) Intuition
- We want max of prices[j] - prices[i] with j > i.
- While scanning from left to right, the best buy for today is simply the minimum price seen so far.
- So at each day, potential profit = today_price - min_so_far. Track the maximum of these.
- This turns a quadratic search into one linear pass by maintaining just two numbers: min_so_far and max_profit.

2) Approach (pseudocode)
- Initialize:
  - min_price = +infinity
  - max_profit = 0
- For each price in prices:
  - min_price = min(min_price, price)              // best buy so far
  - max_profit = max(max_profit, price - min_price) // best sell today vs global best
- Return max_profit

3) Python Solution (heavily commented)
class Solution:
    def maxProfit(self, prices: list[int]) -> int:
        # min_price tracks the lowest price encountered so far (best day to buy up to current day)
        min_price = float('inf')
        # max_profit tracks the best profit found so far
        max_profit = 0

        # Iterate each day's price once
        for price in prices:
            # If current price is a new minimum, update min_price (buy cheaper)
            if price < min_price:
                min_price = price
                # Note: We do not compute profit here because selling on the same day
                # would yield zero profit at best. We'll compute profit on future days.
            else:
                # Otherwise, consider selling today using the best buy so far
                profit = price - min_price
                # Update the best profit if this is better
                if profit > max_profit:
                    max_profit = profit

        # If no profitable transaction exists, max_profit remains 0
        return max_profit

4) Complexity Analysis
- Time: O(n) because we scan the array once, doing O(1) work per element.
- Space: O(1) additional space; we only keep two variables (min_price, max_profit).

Why this is optimal:
- Any solution must inspect each price at least once (to know if a new minimum or a better sell exists), so O(n) time and O(1) space is optimal.

5) Alternative Approaches
- Two-pointer sliding window:
  - Maintain left = index of current minimum (buy), right = current day (sell).
  - If prices[right] >= prices[left], compute profit and move right; else move left to right.
  - Same O(n) time, O(1) space.

- Kadane on differences:
  - Build daily diffs: d[i] = prices[i] - prices[i-1].
  - Max profit equals the maximum subarray sum over d, clamped at 0.
  - Also O(n) time, O(1) space if done on the fly; more abstract but equivalent.

- DP formulation:
  - Let hold = max profit when holding a stock after day i, nohold = max profit when not holding, with at most one transaction.
  - Transitions reduce to the same logic; effectively hold = -min_price, nohold = max_profit.

- Divide and conquer (not recommended here):
  - Compute max profit in left half, right half, and crossing (max_right - min_left).
  - O(n log n) time, O(log n) space due to recursion; inferior to linear scan.
